title: Chapter 14: The Mexican Prop
content: The hallway was cold enough that I could feel it through the soles of my shoes — that Blackwood cold, the kind that seeps up from stone floors like the building itself wants you to remember you're standing on something older and harder than you'll ever be. I was heading back from the kitchen. Late. Past midnight. A glass of water I'd already finished, the empty tumbler sweaty in my hand, and I was thinking about nothing, which was a lie I told myself when I was actually thinking about her.
Robert's study door was cracked open.
I stopped.

Robert Vance's study door was never cracked open. It was never even slightly ajar, never left to chance, because Robert was a man who controlled the position of every object in his orbit. Doors were closed. Cabinets were locked. His daughter was dispatched. His household ran on precision, and a door left open was a failure of order, and Robert did not fail at order.

So the crack — four inches, maybe five, just enough to leak lamplight in a thin blade across the hallway floor — was wrong. It was so wrong that I stood there for a moment just looking at it, the way you'd look at a wound on something that isn't supposed to bleed.
Then I heard my name.

Not "Heath." Not that. It was my surname, clipped and casual, the way Robert always said it — like he was reading an item off a list. Something between a checkmark and a dismissal. And it came through that cracked door in his baritone, amused, the voice he used when he was performing ease for someone whose opinion mattered to him.
I should have kept walking.
I didn't.
I pressed my back against the wall beside the doorframe and listened.

"The Mexican prop is doing his job — diversity numbers are up, the press is satisfied." A pause. A laugh — Robert actually laughed, low and warm, like he was sharing a joke with an old friend. "He'll be gone by next year anyway. They always are."
The hallway got colder.

No — that's not right. The hallway didn't change. I changed. Something inside me dropped through the floor and kept dropping, and the cold I felt was the absence of everything I'd been carrying without knowing I was carrying it. The hope. The small, stupid, animal hope that maybe — maybe — I'd earned something here. That the sweat and the silence and the way I'd made myself useful, made myself small, made myself exactly what this family needed — that it had meant something. That I had meant something.
The Mexican prop.
Prop.

I leaned against the wall and the cold stone bit through my shirt and I didn't move. I couldn't move. My legs had made a decision without consulting my brain, and the decision was stay, listen, let it in, let all of it in, don't you dare flinch.

He said "prop" the way you'd say "chair." The way you'd describe a piece of staging that serves a function and has no feelings about it. A prop doesn't get angry. A prop doesn't hurt. A prop doesn't lie awake at three in the morning clutching a pillow like it's the only thing keeping his ribs from cracking open. A prop just stands where it's placed and does what it's for and then gets struck from the set when the scene changes.
Diversity numbers are up, the press is satisfied.

That's what I was. That's all I'd ever been. Not a scholarship kid who'd earned his place through grit and talent and the kind of hunger that makes you climb walls. Not the boy who'd memorized every exit in this house because his body still believed every room might become a trap. Not even the son Robert never had, which was the fantasy I'd let myself nurse in my weakest moments, watching the way Robert clapped my shoulder at dinner and introduced me to trustees and said "our Heath" like the possessive meant something.
Our Heath. Their prop.

My name — my surname — wasn't a name at all. It was a category. A demographic. A box checked on a compliance form. And Robert was on the phone laughing about it, sharing the punchline with whoever was on the other end, secure in the knowledge that the prop didn't speak, didn't think, didn't bleed.
But that wasn't the part that broke me.
That wasn't the part that reached through my chest and put its fingers around the thing I'd been protecting.
The part that broke me was three words.
*They always are.*
Gone by next year. They always are.
*They.*

Not me. *They.* Plural. Multiple. Before me, there were others. Other props. Other diversity pieces. Other kids who'd been brought into this house and this school and this family's orbit to serve a function and then — what? Gone where? Expelled? Disappeared? Sent back to whatever place Robert had pulled them from, discarded like set dressing between acts?

I thought of Marcus, who'd been in my year when I arrived. A Black kid from Detroit, scholarship, smart as hell. Gone by winter term. The official story was "family emergency," and I'd believed it because why wouldn't I? I thought of Priya, two years ahead, who'd been president of the debate society and then wasn't, and no one talked about it, and I'd thought nothing of it because at Blackwood, people left and you didn't ask why because asking why was a privilege reserved for people who belonged.
I had never belonged.

I had been placed. Positioned. Utilized. And when the next press cycle turned, or the next grant required updated demographics, or the next dinner party needed a showcase of Robert Vance's progressive generosity, there would be another prop. Another *they.* And I would be "gone," and my name would be forgotten, and the grandfather clock in the hallway would keep ticking like I'd never stood next to it at all.
I was still trying to breathe through that — through the expanding nothing in my chest — when Robert's voice changed.
It softened.

He had a voice he used for business, clipped and efficient, and a voice he used for authority, heavy and final, and a voice he used for warmth, which he deployed like a weapon — precisely, only when it could do the most damage. This was the warm voice. Personal. Almost tender.
"She's handling the Linton situation beautifully."
My breath stopped.
"I told you Catherine would come through. She's her mother's daughter — performs anything you script."
I heard the words.
Then I heard what the words meant.
Performs.
Anything.
Script.

Cat was handling Julian. I knew that. We'd planned it together — or I thought we had. The pact. The arrangement. Her job was to keep Julian close, to be the Vance family's inside operator, to play the role everyone expected while we worked our angle in the dark. It was strategy. It was necessity. It was the only move we had on a board where Robert owned every square.
But Robert knew.

Robert knew about the Linton situation. Robert had *told someone* that Cat would come through. Robert had — what? Scripted it? Given her the role? Written the scene and handed her the pages and watched his daughter walk onto the set and perform?

If Cat could perform love for Julian — and she was, I'd watched her do it, I'd watched her smile at that pale-eyed nothing of a boy and touch his arm and laugh at things he said that weren't funny — if she could perform that, then what else could she perform?
What else had she performed?
The pact.

Our pact. The one we'd made in the dark, in the wreckage of our lies and our need and the thing between us that had no name because naming it would make it real and making it real would make it vulnerable and making it vulnerable would give Robert something to break. Our pact — had she written that? Had Robert written that? Had I walked into the scene they'd set, playing my part, hitting my marks, delivering my lines, thinking I was building something when all I was doing was following a script someone else had already drafted?
*She's her mother's daughter.*

I didn't know her mother. Not really. Elaine Vance was a ghost in perfume who glided through rooms and smiled at the right moments and said nothing that could ever be used against her. I'd always thought Cat was different — that beneath her control there was something real, something molten, something that bled when no one was watching. I'd thought I'd seen it. I'd thought I'd felt it, in the way she looked at me when the mask slipped, in the way her voice cracked on my name in the dark.

But that was the trick, wasn't it? The best performance is the one that looks like it isn't a performance at all. The best actors don't seem like they're acting. And if Cat was her mother's daughter — if she could perform anything you scripted — then maybe the crack in her voice wasn't a crack at all. Maybe it was a technique. Maybe the way she looked at me wasn't a window; maybe it was a mirror, reflecting back exactly what I needed to see so I'd stay in position, stay useful, stay *the prop.*
Maybe the pact wasn't ours.
Maybe it was theirs.
Maybe I was never a conspirator. Maybe I was an instrument.

I didn't move. I stood against the wall with the empty glass in my hand and the cold climbing my spine and the two parts of my brain firing simultaneously — one screaming *no, she wouldn't, she couldn't, you felt her, you know what you felt* and the other whispering *you felt what you were supposed to feel, that's what props do, they feel what the scene requires* — and I couldn't tell which voice was the truth and which was the survival instinct trying to protect the last intact thing in my chest.
The phone call ended.

I heard the click of the receiver. The creak of Robert's chair. The shuffle of papers, methodical, contented. Then footsteps — measured, unhurried, the walk of a man who has never once in his life been afraid of what might be waiting on the other side of his own door.
The study door opened.

I moved without thinking. My body pivoted, flattened, pressed itself into the recess beside the grandfather clock — the shadow that the lamplight didn't reach, the dark pocket between the clock's bulk and the wall. I was three feet from Robert Vance, and he didn't see me.
He walked past.

He was wearing his house shoes. That's what I noticed — the soft, leather house shoes that made his footsteps soundless on the stone, and his cufflinks were still in, and his hair was still combed, and he looked like a man who'd just finished a satisfactory business call, which is exactly what it was, because I was his business, Cat was his business, Julian was his business, we were all his business, and business was good.
He turned the corner. The hallway emptied.

I pressed my forehead against the grandfather clock's casing. It was oak, or mahogany, or whatever expensive wood rich men built their timekeeping monuments from. It smelled like polish and age. The pendulum swung behind the glass door beside my hip — tick, tick, tick — measuring the seconds I remained in that shadow, measuring the time I spent not moving, not screaming, not following Robert Vance down that hallway and putting my fist through his throat.
My hands were shaking.

Not the cold. The cold had nothing to do with it. This was the shake of contained detonation, the vibration of something enormous being held in a space too small to contain it. I was vibrating at a frequency that could shatter glass. I was standing in the dark, pressing myself against a clock, and the entire architecture of my life at Blackwood was collapsing in on itself and all I could do was stand there and let the rubble fall.
I set the empty glass on the floor. Carefully. Like it mattered. Like anything mattered.
Then I walked.

Not to my room. Not to the kitchen. Not anywhere the prop was supposed to go at this hour. I walked out of the main house, across the courtyard where the frost was already forming on the flagstones, and I pushed through the chapel door without slowing down.

The chapel at night is a different creature than the chapel in daylight. Daylight, it's all stained glass and reverence and the performative piety of rich children pretending to pray. At night, it's a skeleton. The pews are ribs. The altar is a spine. The windows are black holes. The saints in their niches look less like blessers and more like witnesses to centuries of unconfessed sins.

I walked past our pew — *our* pew, mine and Cat's, the one where we sat side by side in the silence that said more than our mouths ever could — and I didn't look at it. I walked past the altar and the brass candlesticks and the open Bible that someone always left open to the same page, and I went to the wall behind it.
The wall.

I'd been coming here for months. Since the first time Cat's absence had hollowed me out and I couldn't sleep and the only thing that made the noise in my head stop was impact. Kinetic release. Fist against stone, over and over, a one-sided conversation where the wall always won and I always lost and that was fine because losing felt more honest than anything else I was doing at Blackwood.
I hit it.
The first punch was recognition. My fist met stone and the stone said *yes, I know you, you're the one who keeps coming back.*
The second was fury. Knuckles against granite, the sharp kiss of impact, and the pain bloomed bright and immediate and real in a way that absolutely nothing else in my life was real.

The third was confirmation. The skin over my middle knuckle split. I felt it go — the distinct, surgical parting of flesh, the wetness that followed, the warmth of my own blood rising to meet the cold air. I didn't stop.
Again.
Again.
Again.

The wall didn't move. Walls don't move. Walls don't care. That's the thing about hitting stone — it will never hit you back, and it will never reach for you, and it will never perform affection it doesn't feel, because a wall has no feelings to perform. A wall is just a wall. Honest in its opacity. Dependable in its indifference.

My blood was on it now. Smears. Streaks. The stone was pale — some kind of limestone, or sandstone, or whatever ancient bedrock they'd built this chapel on — and the blood showed dark against it, almost black in the low light, and I kept hitting it because the pain was the only thing that didn't lie.

I tasted copper. Salt. The mineral tang of my own body opening against the mineral body of the building, and there was something almost liturgical about it — blood and stone, flesh and earth, the confession that doesn't need words because the penitence is already happening, is already written in the language of split skin and bruised bone.

I hit until the rage ran out of room. That's what it does — it fills you up, it crowds out every other thing, and then when there's nowhere left for it to expand, it has to go somewhere, and the only exit is through the body. Out through the fists. Out through the jaw, which I'd clenched so tight my molars ached. Out through the eyes, and even then I didn't cry. I didn't cry because crying is what props do when they want someone to feel sorry for them, and nobody was going to feel sorry for me, and I wasn't going to ask them to.
I growled. Low, animal, from the chest. The sound bounced off the chapel walls and came back to me distorted, larger, like the building was amplifying my voice because no one else would.

I stood there with my forehead against the wall, breathing. My hands hung at my sides, heavy and wet. The bleeding wasn't bad — split skin, not deep, nothing that wouldn't close — but it was everywhere. My knuckles. My fingers. Running down my palms in thin lines, tracking the creases of my hands like rivers on a map of somewhere I'd never been allowed to go.
I turned around. Slid down the wall until I was sitting on the chapel floor, my back against stone, my legs stretched out in front of me, and I stared at the wall across the aisle.
The graffiti.

Every old building has it — the carvings, the scratchings, the marks that generations of students leave behind like proof they existed. Names. Dates. Obscenities. Small rebellions committed in the margins of sacred space, frozen in stone because even the most surveilled institution can't watch every corner of every room at every hour. Some of the carvings were decades old. I could see them in the thin light that leaked through the chapel windows — initials and arrows and one crude sketch that might have been a middle finger, might have been something worse. Kids like me, once. Props, probably. Diversity pieces. They always are.
They'd left their marks.

I pulled my notebook from my back pocket. The spine was cracked, the pages soft from use. I opened it to a fresh page — the paper white and clean and waiting, the way a confessional waits, the way a wall waits.
I wrote.

My handwriting was bad — my right hand was shaking and the knuckles were swelling and the blood made the pen slippery, but I wrote, because writing was the only thing I'd ever had that couldn't be taken from me, couldn't be scripted, couldn't be performed by someone else wearing my face.
Line 1: *Robert Vance. Everything he's built. Everything he loves. Burn it all.*

I stared at it. The ink was dark against the page, and the words were steady even though my hand wasn't, and they felt like the first true thing I'd said in months. Maybe years. Not a performance. Not a line. Not a prop's dialogue. Mine. From the part of me that was still a boy from a border town who'd learned early that the people with power will always see you as a function before they see you as a person.
Then I wrote the second line. Below the first. And I underlined it twice.
*Including her?*

The question mark was the worst part. Not the question itself — the mark. That little curve and dot, that grammatical admission that I didn't know. That I couldn't answer. That for all my rage and all my blood and all the certainty with which I'd just declared my intention to burn everything Robert Vance had ever built, there was one piece of the fire I couldn't commit to. One name I couldn't add to the list without feeling my own ribs cave in.
Including her?
I didn't answer it.

I sat on the floor of the chapel with blood on my hands and ink on the page and the question hanging in the air between me and every saint carved into the walls, and none of them answered either, because saints are just another kind of prop — placed, posed, expected to perform stillness and grace while everything holy burns around them.
I closed the notebook.
I didn't close the question.

It stayed open the way a wound stays open when you keep picking at it, and I already knew — lying there on the cold chapel floor with my raw hands and my split knuckles and the copper taste still bitter on my tongue — that I would pick at it. That I would keep picking at it until it either healed or killed me, and it wouldn't heal, because the answer was already forming in the dark spaces of my mind like frost on a window, and the answer was: *maybe.*
Maybe including her.
Maybe she was part of it.
Maybe the pact, the looks, the darkness we'd shared, the silence that felt like truth — maybe it was all a performance so good that even the performer had started to believe it.
And maybe it wasn't.
And I would never know.
And that not-knowing — that was the blizzard I was about to walk into.
